title: SEGMENT 12 — Antibodies as drugs — the first wave

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Antibodies as drugs — the first wave
We've spent the last few segments on how antibodies became drugs.
Let me now spend a few minutes on what those drugs are doing, because it's worth grounding the technology in the actual diseases being treated.

I'll walk you through three classic monoclonal antibody therapies — three drugs from the first wave of approvals — because each one illustrates a different therapeutic strategy that the rest of the field has built on.
Rituximab.
Approved in 1997, for non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
The target is a protein called CD20, which is displayed on the surface of B cells — both healthy B cells and the cancerous B cells that make up B cell lymphomas.
Rituximab binds CD20, decorating the lymphoma cell with antibody.

Then, as we discussed in Segment 5, the bound antibody's Fc handles stick out, and the rest of the immune system takes over — natural killer cells grab the Fc and dump cytotoxic granules onto the cell, complement gets activated and punches holes, macrophages eat the antibody-coated cells.
The lymphoma cells die.
The clever choice here, and you should see why this matters, is the target.
CD20 is on B cells, including the cancer ones.
Yes, rituximab also wipes out healthy B cells — that's a side effect — but B cells are replaceable from precursors that don't express CD20, so you bounce back.
And critically, CD20 is not on other essential tissues, so you don't kill anything else.
The drug is targeted because the target is selectively expressed where you want killing.
That's the strategy: find a marker on the disease cell that isn't on essential normal cells, and tag it with an antibody.
Rituximab is a chimeric antibody — the -ximab suffix gives it away — and it was the first major commercial success of monoclonal antibody therapy.
It revolutionized the treatment of B cell lymphomas.
Trastuzumab.
Approved in 1998, for HER2-positive breast cancer.
The target here is HER2, a receptor on the surface of certain breast cancer cells.
About 15-20% of breast cancers overexpress HER2, dramatically — they have ten or twenty times as many copies on their surface as normal cells, and that signaling drives tumor growth.

Trastuzumab binds HER2 and does several things at once: it blocks the receptor from signaling, it flags the cell for immune-mediated destruction through its Fc, and it triggers some direct effects on the cancer cell.
The clinical impact has been enormous — HER2-positive breast cancer used to be among the most aggressive subtypes, and trastuzumab made it one of the most treatable.
Trastuzumab is a humanized antibody — the -zumab tells you — and it illustrates a different strategy from rituximab.
Here, the target isn't a generic marker of a cell type, but specifically a driver of the cancer's growth — a protein the cancer is actually using to proliferate.
Blocking the receptor blocks the cancer's growth signal.
This is the driver-targeting strategy.
Find the molecule the cancer is dependent on, and use an antibody to block it.
Infliximab.
Approved in 1998, for rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease, and other autoimmune conditions.
The target is TNF-alpha — tumor necrosis factor alpha — a small protein your immune cells secrete to drive inflammation.

In autoimmune diseases, TNF-alpha is being produced inappropriately, driving chronic inflammation that damages joints (in rheumatoid arthritis), the gut (in Crohn's), the skin (in psoriasis), and so on.
Infliximab is an antibody that binds TNF-alpha in the bloodstream and tissues, sopping it up, preventing it from reaching its receptors on target cells.
The inflammation calms down.
The strategic point here: rituximab and trastuzumab are killing cells.
Infliximab is doing nothing of the kind — it's just soaking up a soluble messenger molecule so that messenger can't deliver its harmful signal.
The antibody doesn't need an Fc-mediated killing mechanism here; the entire therapeutic effect comes from blocking.
This neutralization strategy works for any soluble protein you want to silence — cytokines, growth factors, toxins.
Sponge it out of circulation with an antibody, the signaling stops, the disease calms down.
Infliximab is chimeric.

Its fully-human cousin adalimumab — Humira — does the same thing with a fully human framework, which is why Humira could be self-injected for years without immunogenicity problems, and why it became the best-selling drug in the world for nearly a decade.
So three drugs, three strategies.
Rituximab: tag the diseased cell with an antibody and let the immune system kill it.
Trastuzumab: block a growth-driving receptor on the disease cell.
Infliximab: soak up a harmful messenger molecule before it can act.
Each of these is a different mode of using an antibody as a drug — recruit killing, block signaling, neutralize a target.
Every modern antibody therapy is some variation, some combination, some refinement of these basic ideas.
Once you see the three modes, the rest of the antibody drug landscape becomes recognizable.

But there's a fourth and fifth wave that's more dramatic still, and that's where we go next: cancer immunotherapy, with the checkpoint inhibitors, and the new generation of antibody constructs — payload-carrying, two-headed, and miniaturized — that are reshaping what antibodies can do.
